coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation and H2 evolution performance of NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction constructed by π-π conjugate self-assembly
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xibao
Luo, Qiuning
Han, Lu
Deng, Fang
Yang, Ya
Dong, Fan
coredata.dc:description: Constructing heterojunction between two semiconductors with matched energy band structure is an effective modification method to obtain excellent photocatalysts. The experimental scheme adopts a simple solvent method to self-assemble nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) on the surface of sulfur doped carbon nitride (S-C3N4) semiconductor through π-π conjugate interaction. Based on this, a novel 0D/2D S-scheme heterojunction NCDs/S-C3N4 hybrid was successfully prepared. The degradation kinetic constants of NCDs/S-C3N4 for rhodamine B (RhB) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) reached 0.23522 and 0.01342 min−1, respectively, which were 2.72 and 2.65 times that of S-C3N4. The highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate was observed under the simulated sunlight irradiation, which was 2.30 times that of S-C3N4. The improvement of photocatalytic performance was mainly based on the formation of the S-scheme heterojunction between S-C3N4 and NCDs. The effects of internal electric field, π-π conjugate interaction and band bending promoted the photogenerated h +and e − with low redox ability to recombine and retained the beneficial h +and e − with strong redox ability, which contributed to the production of more active species of h +and •O2 −, therefore the photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen evolution performance were significantly enhanced.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S-scheme
Internal electric field
Hydrogen evolution
C3N4
π–π interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis scheme of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of samples (a), TEM images of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 (b), HRTEM image of NCDs/S-C3N4 and NCDs (c, d), elemental mapping of C, N, S and O (e).

Photodegradation of RhB under visible-light irradiation (a) and corresponding first-order plots (b), photodegradation of PNP under visible-light irradiation (c) and corresponding first-order plots (d), summary of photocatalytic degradation of RhB (e) and PNP (f) by the prepared samples.
In-situ XPS survey spectra of C 1 s (a), N 1 s (b), S 2p (c), O 1 s (d).
Free radical capture experiments (a), ESR detection results for DMPO- •O2 − (b) and TEMPO- h + (c).

Work function of NCDs (a), S-C3N4 (b), NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 (c), sketches of NCDs and S-C3N4 before/after contact and the formation of IEF and band bending (d), photocatalytic mechanism of NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction (e).

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic degradation and H2 evolution performance of NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction constructed by π-π conjugate self-assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eInstitute of Fundamental and Frontier Sciences, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 611731, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing heterojunction between two semiconductors with matched energy band structure is an effective modification method to obtain excellent photocatalysts. The experimental scheme adopts a simple solvent method to self-assemble nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) on the surface of sulfur doped carbon nitride (S-C3N4) semiconductor through π-π conjugate interaction. Based on this, a novel 0D/2D S-scheme heterojunction NCDs/S-C3N4 hybrid was successfully prepared. The degradation kinetic constants of NCDs/S-C3N4 for rhodamine B (RhB) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) reached 0.23522 and 0.01342 min−1, respectively, which were 2.72 and 2.65 times that of S-C3N4. The highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate was observed under the simulated sunlight irradiation, which was 2.30 times that of S-C3N4. The improvement of photocatalytic performance was mainly based on the formation of the S-scheme heterojunction between S-C3N4 and NCDs. The effects of internal electric field, π-π conjugate interaction and band bending promoted the photogenerated h +and e − with low redox ability to recombine and retained the beneficial h +and e − with strong redox ability, which contributed to the production of more active species of h +and •O2 −, therefore the photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen evolution performance were significantly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is an advanced redox technology, which is widely used in environmental remediation and energy shortage. Specifically, photocatalytic technology can be used to degrade pollutants [1–8], decompose water to produce hydrogen and oxygen [9–17], and reduce CO2 [18–23], etc. However, although photocatalysis technology has made great progress, it still faces great challenges. It mainly includes the following two reasons: high recombination rate of photogenerated h +and e − and low light quantum efficiency.

Consequently, the development of a catalyst with excellent photogenerated carrier separation efficiency and high solar energy utilization is very important for the application of photocatalysts. Porous graphite carbon nitride (pCN) is an organic semiconductor material composed of the abundant C and N elements on the earth, which has received extensive attention [24–27]. However, the original g-C3N4 has some inherent shortcomings, such as the recombination rate of e − and h + is high and the specific surface area is small [28], resulting in still relatively low photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. To enhance the utilization of sunlight and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated h +and e −, researchers have adopted many strategies such as the construction of heterostructure, element doping and the construction of a composite system with carbon materials [26,28,29]. Among them, element doping (such as sulfur) was considered to be an applicable method to enhance photocatalytic performance. According to previous reports, sulfur doping can adjust the band gap and affect its absorption of sunlight. For example, Lv et al. [26] used thiourea as a precursor to prepare sulfur-doped graphite carbon nitride nanosheets. Sulfur doping makes the absorption edge of the photocatalyst blue shift, and the form of S–C–N bond inhibits the recombination of carriers. By controlling the amount of precursor added, the synthesized sulfur-doped carbon nitride has improved photocatalytic activity due to the greater specific surface area, narrower band gap, and stronger reduction of electrons generated by the conduction band. However, although sulfur doped carbon nitride can enhance the utilize range of sunlight, the decrease of band gap leads to the increase of annihilation speed of photogenerated e − and h + and the decrease of oxidation or reduction potential, which are the key factors limiting the reactivity [30]. Heterojunction can promote the separation efficiency of h + and e −, so as to greatly improve the photocatalytic activity. For this reason, it is necessary to construct the heterojunction on the basis of sulfur-doped carbon nitride to further suppress the recombination of photogenerated e − and h +.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nano-scale carbon materials with a size of less than 10 nm. The carbon atoms are sp2/sp3 hybridized and consist of a graphite-like core (sp2 carbon) and an amorphous oxygen-containing shell with various functional groups on the surface [31]. CDs can play a very important role in the field of photocatalysis, such as up-conversion [32], electron acceptor/donor [33], photosensitizer [34], etc. Based on CDs with so many excellent properties, some researchers have successfully combined CDs with g-C3N4 and achieved good photocatalytic performance. Fang et al. used dicyandiamide as starting materials to prepare the hybrid of carbon dots (C-dots) and g-C3N4. Carbon nitride modified by carbon dots hinders the rapid recombination of carriers due to the electron sink effect of carbon dots [35]. Wang et al. successfully prepared modified CDs supported hollow tubular g-C3N4 photocatalysts through ultrasound-assisted methods. CDs acted as photosensitizers and electron mediators [36]. CDs were bonded on the intermediate layer of g-C3N4 successfully through a simple polymerization technique. The photocatalytic reaction rate of the composite of carbon dots with appropriate content and carbon nitride is 13.6 times that of the original carbon nitride. The role of CDs in the composites is mainly the enhancement of the absorption of visible light and the effective promotion of charge separation and transfer [37]. Basically, CDs are used as co-catalysts in the aforementioned photocatalysts. However, the most important thing is that when the CDs are combined with another catalyst, the actual charge migration path of the composite is still unclear, and there is no direct evidence for the migration of e − and h +at the interface between the component semiconductors. For instance, Wang et al. found that during the synthesis of CDs implanted g-C3N4 nanotubes (CCTs), the difference of work function between graphite carbon and g-C3N4 was produced by the microregion heterostructure, which provided high carriers transport dynamics and promoted the separation efficiency of e −-h + pairs. However, the separation and movement speed of e −-h + in CCT complexes reported in this paper were not yet clear [38]. Recent studies have shown that CDs can also act as semiconductors. When graphite becomes C dots, its CB and valence band (VB) are separated, and a quantum confinement effect appears [39–41].

In recent years, due to its unique advantages, S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst has received extensive attention [42–54]. In S-scheme heterojunction system, the recombination of carriers with low redox property is accelerated, but on the contrary, the separation with high redox property is inhibited. Therefore, the construction of S-scheme heterojunction is a useful method to improve the photocatalytic performance. However, for CDs and g-C3N4 systems, theoretical calculations and previous experimental results have reported that g-C3N4 and CDs form a type-II heterojunction [55,56]. As we all know, although the recombination of carriers can be inhibited to a certain extent in type-II heterojunction, it weakens the redox capacity of carriers. Based on the inspiration of previous research ideas, if the S-scheme heterojunction can be directly constructed between g-C3N4 and CDs, it would not only inhibit the recombination of photogenerated h +and e −, but also maintain a high oxidation and reduction potential of photogenerated h + and e −.

In this work, in order to obtain an effective S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, the energy band structure of porous g-C3N4 was modified by the doping of S element. The CDs were doped with N elements to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to promote the coupling with sulfur-doped porous graphite carbon nitride (S-C3N4). Considering the analogous π-conjugated structure of S-C3N4 [57] and NCDs, S-C3N4 and NCDs were combined by π-π conjugate self-assembly strategy to form a S-scheme heterojunction. The photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen evolution properties of NCDs/S-C3N4 were tested. The NCDs/S-C3N4 photocatalyst was characterized by various photoelectric test techniques, the photocatalytic process mechanism and its structure-activity relationship of NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction were discussed.

The raw materials include the following: urea (CH4N2O) (Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China), thioacetamide (TAA) (Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Tianjin, China), citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) (Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Tianjin, China).

A certain amount of urea and distilled water was added to a conical flask and stirred to dissolve (the mass ratio of urea to distilled water is 3:4), and then 50 mg of TAA was added. After sealing the tapered bottle, the temperature of the magnetic heating stirrer was adjusted to 50 °C, and the above solution was heated and stirred for 1 h. Then the mixed solution was transferred to the crucible with cover and kept at 400 °C for 1 h, and then kept at 500 °C for 2 h. The product was sieved with a screen to obtain yellow solid powder of S-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis scheme of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs was synthesized by high pressure hydrothermal synthesis in accordance with the method [37] with appropriate improvements. In short, A certain amount of citric acid and urea (citric acid: urea mass ratio = 3:1) were dissolved in distilled water, and the resulting solution was transferred to a high-pressure hydrothermal reactor equipped with polytetrafluoroethylene lining and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the synthesized brown solution was centrifuged by centrifuge to separate the by-products of large particles. Then the supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da). The resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain NCDs powder.

The preparation process of NCDs/S-C3N4 nanocomposite was as follows: firstly, S-C3N4 was added to distilled water to form a suspension and sonicated using an ultrasonic machine for 15 min. Next, 1 mg NCDs powders was dissolved in distilled water and then sonicated by an ultrasonic machine to make the NCDs solution disperse evenly. The NCDs solution and S-C3N4 suspension were mixed together by stirring overnight, and then stirred at 60 °C to remove the solvent. Finally, the obtained powder was vacuum dried at 70 °C to obtain the composite labeled as NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00. According to the same synthesis route, a series of NCDs/S-C3N4 samples were fabricated with various mass loadings of NCDs (0.25 wt.%, 0.50 wt.%, and 2.00 wt.%). These samples were labeled as NCDs/S-C3N4–0.25, NCDs/S-C3N4–0.50, NCDs/S-C3N4–2.00, respectively.

The phase determination of the materials was detected by the X-ray diffraction with Cu Kα radiation at a 2θ scanning range of 5–80° The morphology of the prepared materials was detected by a transmission electron microscopy. The functional groups of the prepared materials were distinguished by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer of Brvker VERTEX 70. The element composition was analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The UV–Vis DRS was tested by the ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer. The photoelectrochemical properties was studied using a system of three-electrode electrochemical analyzer in electrochemical workstation (Chenhua CHI760E, Shanghai, China). The prepared samples were tested by fluorescence spectrometer to obtain photoluminescence spectra.

The photocurrent density and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were tested by constructing a three-electrode system. The reference electrode and auxiliary electrode are Ag/AgCl and metal Pt sheet, respectively. The carbon paper was used as the working electrode (the area of the carbon paper was 1 × 1.5 cm2), and the surface of the carbon paper was covered with 4 mg of photocatalyst. The working electrode of the three-electrode system was prepared by the following preparation method: firstly, carbon paper surface was ultrasonically clean for a certain time using ethanol and distilled water, respectively. The carbon paper surface was etched using concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 18 M) and finally distilled water was used to ultrasonically clean the carbon paper for 15 min. The carbon paper is dried in an oven at 100 °C after etching and washing. A certain amount of photocatalyst was dissolved in isopropanol and distilled water (volume ratio 1:1) to form a suspension. Then the suspension was evenly coated on the etched carbon paper, with a coating area of 1 × 1 cm2. Finally, the coated carbon paper was dried at 50 °C to form the working electrode. In this three-electrode system, 0.5 M L −1 Na2SO4 was selected as the electrolyte. A xenon lamp with 300 W (420 nm<λ<780 nm) was used as simulated visible light source.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB and PNP with a concentration of 10 mg L −1 was conducted under simulated visible light irradiation. Firstly, the linear distance between the liquid level of the pollutant solution and the xenon lamp was 15 cm. Next, 100 mg photocatalyst was mixed with 100 mL prepared RhB or PNP solution to obtain suspension stirring in an environment without light for a certain time to reach physical adsorption equilibrium. In the process of photocatalytic degradation experiment, the quantitative suspension was taken at the same time interval and centrifuged by centrifuge to obtain the supernatant, which was monitored by a high performance liquid chromatography to record the concentration. Similarly, photocatalytic water splitting was carried out to assess the hydrogen evolution activity. 50 mg photocatalyst, 90 mL distilled water, 10 mL triethanolamine and 1.0 wt% Pt were added to a sealing system. To eliminate the influence of hydrogen in the air, the process of hydrogen production was conducted without oxygen. The temperature of aqueous solution will increase in the process of illumination by xenon lamp. Based on the fact that the effect of hydrogen production is related to temperature, the experimental temperature in the process of photocatalytic water splitting was controlled at 6 °C. Argon with high purity was used as carrier gas, and the amount of hydrogen produced was detected by gas chromatography (GC-7890b Aglient) every 0.5 h.

The active species were detected by radical capture experiment. •O2 −, •OH, and h + were detected respectively by capture reagent of benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO). The JEOL JES-FA200 ESR Spectrometer was used to conduct the ESR experiments. The trapping agents of •O2 − and h +were DMPO and TEMPO, respectively.

0D/2D NCDs/S-C3N4 heterostructures were prepared by a solvothermal evaporation method. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The morphology of the synthesized photocatalyst was observed by TEM. The morphology of NCDs/S-C3N4 is a two-dimensional porous structure in favor of adsorbing more pollutants [58] (Fig. 1(b)). Besides, the introduction of the channel can greatly promote the penetration of light energy into the sample [59]. Fig. 1(c) shows that the lattice spacing of NCDs attached to the surface of S-C3N4 is 0.341 nm, which belongs to the (002) planes of NCDs [60]. The lattice spacing of 0.330 nm can be corresponded to the (002) planes of S-C3N4 [61]. Fig. 1(d) displays the HRTEM image of NCDs with a diameter of about 5 nm. Moreover, the existence of hybridization was further proved by EDS elemental analysis. The element mapping further exhibits that all elements are uniformly distributed in the NCDs/S-C3N4 sample (Fig. 1(e)). Furthermore, the presence of S element confirms the successful introduction of S in S-C3N4, while the presence of O element indicates that NCDs is included in the NCDs/S-C3N4 composite. Therefore, these results illustrate that NCDs are successfully self-assembled on porous S-C3N4 nanosheets, which may arise from the π–π conjugate interactions [62].

Phase identification of the synthesized photocatalyst was detected by XRD. Fig. S1 shows a series of XRD pattern of the prepared samples. The characteristic peaks at 13.1° and 27.5° belong to the planes of (100) and (002) of g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526), respectively. Similarly, the XRD image of S-C3N4 also shows two characteristic peaks (Fig.S1(a)). The strong diffraction peak located at 27.52° belongs to the (002) lattice spacing related to inter-layer stacking with the conjugated ring. Another weaker diffraction peak at 13.10° belongs to the (100) crystal plane, which corresponds to the in-plane stacking structure of tri-s-triazine unit [63,64]. This indicates that S-C3N4 doped with S element does not destroy the structure of g-C3N4. According to the Bragg equation 2dsinθ=λ, the lattice spacing of the (100) and (002) plane is 0.675 and 0.324 nm, respectively. In addition, the XRD pattern of NCDs in Fig. S1(b) displays a broad peak at 25.90° with a lattice spacing of 0.37 nm, which is larger than that of graphite [65] . The slightly increase could be caused by the involvement of N doping and oxygen containing groups [66]. The XRD results of NCDs/S-C3N4 and S-C3N4 are basically the same, indicating that the coupling of NCDs and S-C3N4 will not destroy the crystal structure of S-C3N4. Besides, the diffraction intensity of (002) peak in NCDs/S-C3N4 composite decreases with the content increase of NCDs, indicating that the stacking (crystallization) of S-C3N4 nanosheets are affected due to the π-π conjugate interaction between S-C3N4 and NCDs [67]. In addition, the local enlarged image of S-C3N4 and NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is shown in the Fig. S1(b). It is obvious that the diffraction angle of (002) peak of S-C3N4 moves from 27.52° to 27.20° after the introduction of NCDs, which indicates that the strong interaction between S-C3N4 and NCDs leads to the increase of lattice spacing [68]. Due to the low crystallinity and low content of NCDs, no diffraction peak of NCDs is observed in NCDs/S-C3N4 compounds.

The interaction between the S-C3N4 and NCDs interface was also researched by FT-IR spectra. The characteristic peak at 813 cm−1 is originated from the breathing vibration pattern of the triazine units (Fig. S2), indicating that the tri-s-triazine of S-C3N4 is preserved in the composite of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 [69]. The peaks between 1200 with 1700 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of S-C3N4 ring. In the NCDs spectra, the sharp peaks at 1698, 1650, 1572, and 1400 cm−1 correspond to C = O, C = N, NH, and CN, respectively [70–71]. The broad peak at 3430 cm−1 is belong to COH and NH [37]. Interestingly, several bands characteristic for the NCDs/S-C3N4 heterocycle stretching vibration show a small blue shift compared to the pure S-C3N4, indicating the strong interaction between NCDs and S-C3N4. This result indicates that NCDs is successfully loaded on S-C3N4 and the π-π conjugate interaction between S-C3N4 and NCDs is responsible for the self-assembly of NCDs/S-C3N4 composites [35,62].

The photodegradation ability of the prepared samples for RhB and PNP and the ability to generate hydrogen were tested under simulated sunlight irradiation (Fig. 2 ). Fig. 2(a) shows that the photocatalytic activity of S-C3N4 and NCDs is relatively poor, which may be due to the faster recombination rate of single semiconductor [72]. However, with the increase of NCDs content, the photocatalytic degradation activity increases. When the content of NCDs is 1.00% in NCDs/S-C3N4, 99% of RhB was degraded by NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 within 15 min. However, only 71% of RhB was degraded by S-C3N4 within 15 min. This shows that the addition of NCDs improves the photocatalytic performance of S-C3N4. However, further increasing the content of NCDs (>1.00 wt.%) will weaken the photocatalytic activity, this is partly because the excessive addition of NCDs hinders the absorption of visible light by S-C3N4, thereby reducing the formation of active species [37]. To verify the versatility of the prepared catalyst for pollutant degradation, p-nitrophenol (PNP) in phenols is degraded by photocatalyst of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00. Fig. 2(c) shows that the degradation rate of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 over PNP reaches 92% while the degradation rate of S-C3N4 is only 63% within 180 min. The degradation kinetics of RhB and PNP was analyzed by pseudo first-order kinetic curve. Fig. 2(b) and (d) show the corresponding kinetic fitting curves of the degradation of RhB and PNP. In order to describe the kinetic constant (k value) directly, the histograms are shown in Fig. 2(e) and (f). The kinetics equation of RhB degradation shows that the k value of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 reaches 0.23522 min−1, which is 2.72 times than that of S-C3N4 (Fig. 2(e)). Fig. 2(f) shows that the k value of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 for degrading PNP is 0.01342 min−1, which is 2.65 times that of S-C3N4. With the further increase of the relative content of NCDs, the photocatalytic degradation rate of NCDs/S-C3N4 complex becomes lower, which may be due to the excessive NCDs content affecting the simulated sunlight absorption of S-C3N4. It indicates that the successful construction of NCDs/S-C3N4 heterojunction reveals a synergetic catalytic effect of the NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 composites and significantly improves the photocatalytic degradation performance.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of RhB and PNP by NCDs/S-C3N4 was compared with other published papers in order to analyze the photocatalytic performance more objectively, as shown in Table S1, it can be seen that NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 possesses excellent photocatalytic degradation in this experiment. In order to verify the photocatalytic degradation stability of the catalyst, a cycle experiment of degrading RhB was repeated three times. Fig. S3(a) shows the photocatalytic degradation performance of RhB by NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is basically the same, and the degradation efficiency can still reach 91% within 15 min. Besides, Fig. S3(b) displays the XRD patterns of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 before and after photocatalysis for three cycles did not change significantly, which indicated that NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 demonstrated relatively stable photocatalytic activity and structural stability during the process of photocatalysis.

Water splitting ability of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 was tested by photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiment. Fig. S4(a) shows that the hydrogen evolution rate of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is about 483.76 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 2.3 times that of S-C3N4. The improvement of hydrogen evolution efficiency of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 photocatalyst might be attributed to the successful preparation of heterojunction, which improves the separation ability of photogenerated h + and e − in NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 was measured using Xenon lamp at centered wavelength of 420 nm. The AQE is calculated by the following formula [69]:
AQE
( % )
=
2
×
M
×
N A
×
h
× c
S
×
P
×
t
×
λ
×
100 %
where M is the molar mass of hydrogen produced, N A is the Avogadro constant, c is the light speed, h is the Planck constant, P is the light intensity, S is the irradiation area, t is the time of hydrogen evolution, λ is the wavelength. The irradiation area of light is about 23 cm2. The time of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution is 1 h. After calculation, the apparent quantum efficiency of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 at 420 nm is 4.67%.

To verify the HER stability of the prepared material, three cycle experiments were carried out. Fig. S4(b) shows that NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 reaches a better photocatalytic HER activity than that of S-C3N4 and NCDs. By comparing the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 with other reported materials (Table S2), the activity of hydrogen production of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is at a relatively good level.

The photoelectrochemical properties of S-C3N4, NCDs and NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 were characterized under simulated sunlight irradiation. The photocatalyst on the working electrode would produce a photoelectric effect to form a photocurrent when the working electrode was irradiated by xenon lamp [73]. The intensity of photocurrent reflected the recombination velocity of photogenerated h +and e −. It can be found that the curves appear jitter after light irradiation, which can be attributed to the interference of background light and the rotation of the rotor [74] (Fig. S5(a)). The photocurrent of the S-C3N4 and NCDs is weak, while the NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 compound shows enhanced photocurrent response. The reason may be that the photogenerated carriers produced by the original S-C3N4 or NCDs recombine faster, and therefore shows a weaker photocurrent. However, due to the successful construction of heterojunction and the appropriate addition of NCDs, the e −-h +pairs can be separated effectively, therefore, the NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 composite shows the strongest photocurrent, which is consistent with the results of photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production experiments.

The EIS experiments were conducted to study the transfer ability of photogenerated e − and h + of the samples. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy consists of two regions. The electron migration process is relevant to the high-frequency region. The smaller the radius of the arc, the smaller the impedance, which will be more conducive to the charge transfer process [75]. As shown in Fig. S5b, the impedance arc of pure S-C3N4 is relatively large, indicating that the charge transfer ability of pure S-C3N4 is poor. After being combined with NCDs, the impedance arc of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is smaller than that of S-C3N4, indicating that the addition of NCDs to S-C3N4 is conducive to the efficient electron transfer at the heterojunction interface

Subsequently, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) experiments were performed on the samples to study the recombination rate of h +and e −. The PL intensity is relevant to the recombination rate of h +-e − pairs. When 365 nm light was used to excite S-C3N4, a strong emission signal was obtained at about 470 nm (Fig. S5(c)), which is due to the faster recombination of h +and e −. The emission peak of NCDs was obtained at about 500 nm when 365 nm light was used to excite it. However, the PL intensity of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is significantly reduced compared to that of S-C3N4, indicating that the successful coupling of NCDs and S-C3N4 promotes charge transfer.

The band structure of semiconductors is closely related to the performance of photocatalytic materials. Fig. S6(a) exhibits the UV–Vis DRS diagram of the samples. Compared with S-C3N4, the absorption edge of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 exhibits a slight red shift. This is caused by the electronic coupling and quantum effects of NCDs [76], indicating that the heterojunction was successfully formed [77]. At the same time, the absorbance of the composite photocatalyst in the visible light area is enhanced, which is conducive to capture more sunlight. In order to further study the optical properties of S-C3N4 and NCDs, the bandgap (E g) was calculated by Tauc method [58,69,78]. According to the Tauc method, the E g values of S-C3N4 and NCDs was calculated as 2.44 and 1.38 eV, respectively (Fig. S6(b)).

The valence band potential (E VB-XPS) of the semiconductor was measured by VB-XPS over a small range. Fig. S6(c) and (d) show the VB-XPS spectra of S-C3N4 and NCDs. The oblique line near the 0 position of the X axis is used as the tangent, and the value corresponding to the intersection is the valence band potential. By reading the intersection in Fig. S6(c) and (d), it can be confirmed that the E VB-XPS of S-C3N4 and NCDs are 0.58 and 1.49 eV, respectively. Since there is a potential difference between the sample and the instrument, the standard hydrogen electrode potential (E VB-NHE) was calculated using the formula [58,69]: E VB-NHE=φ+E VB-XPS-4.44, where φ is the instrument work function of the XPS analyzer (4.55 eV). Therefore, the E VB-NHE of S-C3N4 and NCDs can be obtained as 0.69 and 1.60 eV, respectively. Finally, the E CB of S-C3N4 and NCDs is identified as −1.75 and 0.22 eV, respectively.

In-situ XPS photoelectron spectroscopy was employed to analyze the element composition and determine the photoelectron transfer direction at the heterojunction interface based on the change of binding energy [58]. A xenon lamp with 300 W (420 nm<λ<780 nm) was placed at a position about 25 cm higher than the tested sample, the changes of the electron binding energy of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 were researched under simulated visible light irradiation. Fig. 3 (a) shows the C 1 s peak may be divided into three peaks, containing C = C (284.8 eV), C = N (286.2 eV) and N–C = N (288.2 eV), respectively. For N 1 s, it may be split into three peaks, including C = N–C (398.8 eV), N–(C)3 (400.27 eV) and C–N–H (401.3 eV) (Fig. 3(b)). Significantly, due to the doping amount of S element is relatively small, a crude S 2p XPS spectrum can be obtained. S 2p may be divided into two peaks, containing C-S (163.73 eV) and S-O (168.24 eV), respectively (Fig. 3(c)). For O 1 s in the NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 composite, most of the O elements come from NCDs, and only a few O elements come from the adsorbed water on the surface of the material. O 1 s could be divided into two peaks, containing CO (532.04 eV) and C = O (533.18 eV), respectively. The existence of CO and C = O bonds verifies the successful incorporation of NCDs onto S-C3N4 (Fig. 3(d)). Interestingly, the characteristic peaks of N 1 s, S 2p and O 1 s in the in-situ XPS of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 before and after illumination shift. In the N1s spectrum, the electron binding energy of the N-(C)3 bonds decreases by 0.22 eV after irradiation, which indicates that the electron density of S-C3N4 increases. For S 2p spectrum, the electron binding energy of the C-S bonds existed in S-C3N4 decreases by 0.21 eV after irradiation, which indicates that the electron density increases. However, for O 1 s spectrum, the electron binding energy of C = O bonds increases by 0.24 eV after irradiation, manifesting the electron density of NCDs decreases. The results not only show that the migration direction of photogenerated e − is from NCDs to S-C3N4 when NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is excited by simulated visible light, but also indicate that the NCDs/S-C3N4 heterojunction is successfully constructed.

To better understand the photocatalytic degradation of RhB/PNP and hydrogen production mechanism of the experimental system of NCDs/S-C3N4, the active species capture experiment was conducted. NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 with the best photocatalytic activity was used as photocatalyst to degrade RhB. BQ, AO, and IPA were added as capture agents of ·O2 −, h +, and ·OH. In the blank control group without any capture agent, the degradation of RhB could reach 97% when photocatalysis was conducted for 15 min. When BQ and AO were added during the experiment, the degradation of RhB in the same 15 min was only 22% and 59%, respectively. However, IPA had little degradation effect on RhB, which indicated ·OH did not play a role in the photocatalytic process (Fig. 4 (a)). From the above results of active species capture experimental, it can be inferred that h + and ·O2 −are mainly the active species in the photocatalytic process. Electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment is also a feasible method to detect the active species. Fig. 4(b) reveals that there is no DMPO-·O2 − signal in the dark, but there is a strong DMPO-·O2 − signal in the light, which indicates that ·O2 − is the active substance. Fig. 4(c) exhibits the TEMPO-h + signal in the dark, while the signal of TEMPO-h + decreases under light irradiation, indicating h + is also the active specie. The free radical capture and ESR experiment results are consistent with the possible photocatalytic mechanism.

Determining the work function between semiconductor materials is very important for analyzing the charge transfer direction at the material interface, which provides a theoretical basis. Fig. 5 (a-c) shows the work function of S-C3N4, NCDs and NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00, respectively, which is measured by VB-XPS. When the materials have intimate contact with each other, materials with larger work function are easy to get electrons, while materials with smaller work function are easy to lose electrons until the fermi energy levels of the two materials reach the same level. Thus, the material surface with small work function is positively charged, and the material surface with large work function is positively charged. According to the formula of Φ=ΔV+φ (Φ is the work function of the sample,φ is 4.55 eV) [58,69,78], ΔV is obtained from the distance between IP1 and IP2 (IP1 is the change point of binding energy relative to the baseline, and IP2 is the midpoint of the corresponding Fermi edge curve) (Fig. 5(a-c)), and then Φ is calculated. The distances between the two IPs of NCDs, S-C3N4 and NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 are 1.24, 0.76, and 0.98 eV, respectively, therefore, the work function of NCDs, S-C3N4 and NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 is 5.79, 5.31, and 5.53 eV, respectively. Fig. 5(d) shows that the work function of NCDs is larger than that of S-C3N4. When NCDs and S-C3N4 come into contact closely, electrons tend to migrate from the surface of S-C3N4 with higher fermi level (E F2) to the surface of NCDs with lower E F1 for achieving the E F balance between them. Then, an internal electric field directed from S-C3N4 to NCDs and the band bending were formed at the interface. Driven by the IEF and band bending effect, the recombination of photogenerated e − on the CB of NCDs and photogenerated h + on the VB of S-C3N4 would be accelerated when NCDs/S-C3N4 is irradiated by simulated visible light. In short, due to the matched band structure, the repulsion of IEF and the influence of band bending, carriers with strong redox ability are retained.

The possible photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production mechanism of NCDs/S-C3N4 heterojunction was proposed (Fig. 5(e)). It can be known from the in-situ XPS analysis that the e − migrates from NCDs to S-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 5(e), due to the significantly different work functions of S-C3N4 and NCDs, an IEF and the band bending are formed on interface when S-C3N4 and NCDs come into close contact. The photogenerated e − migrates from NCDs to S-C3N4 on account of the influence of the IEF and the band bending when NCDs/S-C3N4 is excited by simulated visible light. The h + with oxidation activity on NCDs and the electrons with strong reducing activity on S-C3N4 could be retained. Therefore, the NCDs/S-C3N4 heterojunction not only effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers, but also retain the redox capacity of carriers. In the NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 heterojunction system, since the conduction band potential of S-C3N4 is sufficiently negative (−1.75 eV), the photogenerated e − on S-C3N4 could react with dissolved oxygen to form •O2 − (E(O2/•O2 −) = −0.33 eV vs. NHE). Similarly, due to the repulsion of the IEF, the h + produced by NCDs (1.60 eV) could not be transferred to S-C3N4. Therefore, •O2 − and h + are involved in the degradation of RhB and PNP. ·OH cannot be detected because the valence band potential (1.60 eV) in the VB of NCDs is not enough to oxidize OH−to ·OH (OH−/·OH, 1.99–2.29 eV). This is consistent with the experimental results of free radical capture. Owing to the CB edge of S-C3N4 is −1.75 eV, the e − on the CB of S-C3N4 is responsible for hydrogen evolution. Finally, based on the analysis of band structure, work function and active groups detection results of NCDs/S-C3N4, it is considered that the photocatalytic mechanism of the experimental system of NCDs/S-C3N4 is S-scheme heterojunction mechanism.

In conclusion, 0D/2D NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction materials were successfully synthesized by solvent evaporation. S-C3N4 and NCDs were self-assembled together through π-π conjugate interaction. In situ XPS, work function and ESR experimental analysis provided strong evidence for elucidating the charge transfer path of the NCDs/S-C3N4 heterojunction materials in the photocatalytic process. When excited by light, the NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction materials produce carriers with strong redox ability and excellent separation and migration ability, which greatly improves the photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen evolution performance. The degradation kinetic constants of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 for RhB and PNP were 2.72 and 2.65 times that of S-C3N4, respectively. The hydrogen production rate of NCDs/S-C3N4–1.00 was 2.3 times that of S-C3N4. The active substance capture experiments and ESR experiments proved that the active groups were mainly h +and •O2 − during the process of photocatalysis. This work provides a feasible scheme for the successful synthesis of 0D/2D NCDs/S-C3N4 S-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic materials by solvent evaporation induced self-assembly. The carriers produced by S-scheme heterojunction have strong redox, separation and migration ability, and finally significantly improve the photocatalytic degradation and hydrogen production performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51962023, 51772139), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province, China (No. 20192ACBL21047, 20212BAB204045), the Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle (Nanchang Hangkong University) (No. ES202002077) and the 2019 Project of Liaoning Education Department (No. 2019LNJC20).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2021.10.030.
